description:
A senior researcher has handed you a partially-organised workspace at
    
        /home/user/research_datasets/
    
    containing three already-prepared datasets.  Each dataset lives in its own
    sub-directory and includes a small JSON metadata file named
    <dataset_dir>/meta.json with the following guaranteed keys:
    
        {
          "description": "<human readable text>",
          "rows": <positive integer>,
          "columns": <positive integer>
        }
    
    Your goal is to write concise Markdown documentation for every dataset
    that currently exists inside /home/user/research_datasets and to record a
    very small lint report proving that your Markdown file is free of the most
    common stylistic problems.

    Expected deliverables (all paths must be **exactly** as shown):

        1.  /home/user/research_datasets/docs/README.md
            •  Must start with the level-1 heading:  "# Research Datasets"
            •  Immediately followed by a blank line.
            •  Must contain a four-column Markdown table in this exact order:
                   | Dataset | Rows | Columns | Description |
              The header row must be followed by a divider row made only of
              dashes and pipes (markdown-standard `| --- |` style).
              Afterwards, one row per dataset directory that appears directly
              under /home/user/research_datasets/, sorted **alphabetically by
              directory name**.  Use the directory’s basename as the Dataset
              column, and pull the remaining values from its meta.json.
            •  After the table, include a blank line and a level-2 heading
               whose text begins exactly with:
                   "## Documentation generated:"
               followed by a single space and an ISO-8601 UTC timestamp
               (e.g. 2025-08-17T13:44:02Z).  No other text may appear on this
               line.  The timestamp must reflect the moment you generate the
               file (±2 min is acceptable).

        2.  /home/user/research_datasets/docs/lint_report.log
            •  The file must be plain text.
            •  It must contain exactly one line:
                   "README.md OK - no lint issues detected"
              if and only if all of the following conditions are true:
                   – README.md contains no TAB characters.
                   – No line in README.md ends with trailing whitespace.
                   – README.md ends with a single newline character.
            •  If any of the three checks fail, list one problem per line in
              the format:
                   "<line-number>: <description of problem>"
              (line numbers start at 1).  The automated test will accept *either*
              the single “OK” line or one or more well-formed problem lines.

    Important implementation notes:

        •  Keep all work inside /home/user/research_datasets; do not move files.
        •  You are free to use any shell tools or write short scripts (bash,
          python, awk, etc.) as long as they run without root privileges.
        •  Make sure the docs/ directory exists before you create the files.
        •  The entire task should take no more than 5–10 terminal commands.
        •  When you are done, the two files above must exist and exactly match
          the format described here so that the automated tester can verify
          your work.
truth:
Existing directory tree *before* the agent starts:

      /home/user/research_datasets/
      ├── climate_data/
      │   ├── data.csv
      │   └── meta.json     (contents shown below)
      ├── ocean_salinity/
      │   ├── salinity.csv
      │   └── meta.json     (contents shown below)
      ├── star_catalog/
      │   ├── stars.parquet
      │   └── meta.json     (contents shown below)

    Exact contents of the three metadata files:

    /home/user/research_datasets/climate_data/meta.json
    ---------------------------------------------------
    {
      "description": "Daily world surface temperature readings.",
      "rows": 36500,
      "columns": 8
    }

    /home/user/research_datasets/ocean_salinity/meta.json
    -----------------------------------------------------
    {
      "description": "Global ocean surface salinity measurements.",
      "rows": 54000,
      "columns": 6
    }

    /home/user/research_datasets/star_catalog/meta.json
    ---------------------------------------------------
    {
      "description": "Stellar positions and magnitudes from deep-sky survey.",
      "rows": 112000,
      "columns": 12
    }

    What the automated checker will verify after the agent finishes:

        1. File /home/user/research_datasets/docs/README.md exists.
        2. First non-empty line is exactly "# Research Datasets".
        3. A well-formed Markdown table appears with *exactly* these three
           data rows in the order: climate_data, ocean_salinity, star_catalog.
           The numeric values must match the numbers above, and the Description
           column text must match the meta.json "description" verbatim.
        4. After the table and one blank line, a level-2 heading begins with
           "## Documentation generated: " followed by an ISO-8601 time string
           matching the regexp
              ^## Documentation generated: [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$
           (The checker ignores the actual timestamp value.)
        5. README.md has no TAB characters, no trailing whitespace, ends with a
           single newline, and contains no extra lines after the timestamp.
        6. File /home/user/research_datasets/docs/lint_report.log exists.
        7. If README.md passes all three lint rules, the log must be exactly
           the single line:
               "README.md OK - no lint issues detected\n"
           Otherwise, the log must include one or more lines matching the
           pattern
               ^[0-9]+: .+$
           and none of those lines may be the “OK” line.

    The testing harness will **not** check the exact sequence of commands the
    agent used—only the final filesystem state and file contents described
    above.
